Weekly Course Schedule

Week 1: Welcome to Nursing: Identity, Purpose, and the Practical Nurse Role

Focus: Students begin forming a professional nursing identity by examining what nursing is, why it matters, and how practical nurses contribute to safe, compassionate care.

Nursing leaders: Florence Nightingale and Clara Barton

Topics: Course orientation and expectations; What nursing meant historically and what nursing means today; The practical nurse as caregiver, communicator, advocate, team member, and lifelong learner; Florence Nightingale's work in sanitation, observation, data, and reform; Clara Barton's service, disaster response, and humanitarian leadership.

Activity: Class discussion: 'Why nursing?' Students write a one-page professional beginning reflection on the kind of nurse they hope to become.

Assessment: Professional Beginning Reflection

Week 2: Nursing Then and Now: Caregiving, Reform, Education, and Public Trust

Focus: Students compare nursing in earlier eras with today's profession and discuss how access, race, gender, public health, and education shaped the field.

Nursing leaders: Mary Seacole, Mary Eliza Mahoney, and Lillian Wald

Topics: Nursing before formal schools and licensing; Hospital training schools, community health, and the growth of professional standards; Mary Seacole's battlefield care and resilience; Mary Eliza Mahoney as the first professionally trained Black nurse in the United States; Lillian Wald, public health nursing, settlement work, and care for vulnerable communities.

Activity: Small-group timeline activity comparing 'nursing back then' with 'nursing today' across education, safety, documentation, and patient voice.

Assessment: Quiz 1: Weeks 1-2

Week 3: The Purpose of Nursing: Caring, Comfort, Safety, Advocacy, and Healing

Focus: Students examine nursing as both skilled work and human service, with attention to caring, basic needs, comfort, dignity, safety, and advocacy.

Nursing leaders: Virginia Henderson and Jean Watson

Topics: Nursing as care of the whole person; Basic needs, independence, comfort, and dignity; Therapeutic presence, active listening, and respectful communication; Virginia Henderson's definition of nursing and patient independence; Jean Watson's caring theory and the human side of care.

Activity: Role-play therapeutic responses to anxious, embarrassed, angry, and grieving patients. Students identify responses that protect dignity.

Assessment: Therapeutic Communication Practice

Week 4: The Health Care Team: Scope, Collaboration, Delegation, and Communication

Focus: Students learn how practical nurses fit within the health care team, including collaboration with RNs, providers, nursing assistants, and other disciplines.

Nursing leaders: Isabel Hampton Robb and Mildred Montag

Topics: Practical nurse role and limits of student practice; Teamwork, delegation, assignment, and supervision; SBAR communication and closed-loop communication; Isabel Hampton Robb and nursing education standards; Mildred Montag and the development of associate degree nursing education.

Activity: SBAR practice lab using short patient scenarios. Students identify what to report, who to report to, and what information must be documented.

Assessment: Quiz 2: Weeks 3-4

Week 5: Ethics in Nursing: Values, Boundaries, Confidentiality, and Patient Rights

Focus: Students apply beginning ethical principles to everyday nursing situations involving dignity, truthfulness, privacy, fairness, and professional boundaries.

Nursing leaders: Lavinia Dock and Mabel Keaton Staupers

Topics: Autonomy, beneficence, nonmaleficence, justice, fidelity, veracity, and accountability; Confidentiality and privacy as ethical duties; Professional boundaries and social media caution; Patient rights, dignity, and respect for cultural/religious values; Lavinia Dock's advocacy and Mabel Keaton Staupers' civil rights leadership in nursing.

Activity: Ethics case discussion: A student sees a classmate post about a patient on social media. Students identify the ethical issues and appropriate actions.

Assessment: Ethics Case Response

Week 6: Legal Foundations: Scope of Practice, Documentation, Privacy, and Accountability

Focus: Students learn the legal foundations that guide safe beginning practice and prepare for the midterm exam.

Nursing leaders: Dorothea Dix and Susie Walking Bear Yellowtail

Topics: Scope of practice and student nurse limitations; HIPAA, confidentiality, and protected health information; Informed consent, refusal of care, incident reporting, and mandatory reporting; Documentation basics: timely, factual, objective, complete, and corrected appropriately; Dorothea Dix's mental health advocacy and Susie Walking Bear Yellowtail's advocacy for Native American health.

Activity: Documentation correction exercise using sample notes. Students identify objective wording, missing facts, and unsafe language.

Assessment: Midterm Exam: Weeks 1-6

Week 7: Culture, Health Equity, and Respectful Care

Focus: Students explore cultural humility, social determinants of health, bias, and the nurse's role in respectful, equitable care.

Nursing leaders: Madeleine Leininger and Hazel W. Johnson-Brown

Topics: Culture, religion, language, family roles, and health beliefs; Social determinants of health and barriers to care; Bias, assumptions, and respectful curiosity; Interpreter use and plain-language communication; Madeleine Leininger's transcultural nursing and Hazel W. Johnson-Brown's leadership as an Army nurse and educator.

Activity: Cultural humility scenario workshop. Students practice asking respectful questions without making assumptions.

Assessment: Health Equity Reflection

Week 8: Safety, Quality, Infection Prevention, and the Nurse's Watchful Eye

Focus: Students connect nursing observation, safety habits, quality improvement, and infection prevention to patient outcomes.

Nursing leaders: Linda Richards and Ildaura Murillo-Rohde

Topics: Patient identification, fall prevention, hand hygiene, standard precautions, and infection prevention; Quality improvement, near misses, incident reports, and a just culture mindset; Observation and reporting as core nursing work; Linda Richards and the development of nursing documentation; Ildaura Murillo-Rohde and advocacy for Hispanic nurses.

Activity: Safety walk-through: students identify hazards in a simulated room and explain what action a beginning practical nursing student should take.

Assessment: Quiz 3: Weeks 7-8

Week 9: Introduction to the Nursing Process and Clinical Judgment

Focus: Students learn the beginning language of nursing thinking: noticing, collecting data, reporting changes, planning basic care, and evaluating outcomes.

Nursing leaders: Ida Jean Orlando and Patricia Benner

Topics: Assessment/data collection, nursing diagnosis concepts, planning, implementation, and evaluation; Clinical judgment: noticing, interpreting, responding, and reflecting; Prioritization basics and when to ask for help; Ida Jean Orlando's nursing process theory; Patricia Benner's novice-to-expert model.

Activity: Case mapping exercise: students identify what they noticed, what it might mean, what they would report, and what safe care actions may follow.

Assessment: Clinical Judgment Worksheet

Week 10: Patient Teaching, Health Promotion, and Community Impact

Focus: Students discuss how practical nurses make an impact through teaching, prevention, maternal-child health, chronic disease support, and community service.

Nursing leaders: Mary Breckinridge and Margaret Sanger

Topics: Health promotion and illness prevention across the lifespan; Teach-back, health literacy, and plain-language education; Community nursing, maternal-child care, chronic disease support, and prevention; Mary Breckinridge and frontier nursing; Margaret Sanger's influence on reproductive health education and the need to discuss controversial history with professionalism.

Activity: Teaching micro-lab: students create and deliver a three-minute plain-language teaching script using teach-back.

Assessment: Quiz 4: Weeks 9-10

Week 11: Professionalism, Resilience, Leadership, and Lifelong Learning

Focus: Students prepare for the responsibilities of nursing school and practice by focusing on professionalism, feedback, resilience, leadership, and growth.

Nursing leaders: Mary Adelaide Nutting and Luther Christman

Topics: Professional appearance, punctuality, communication, accountability, and integrity; Managing stress, asking for help, and building safe learning habits; Feedback, remediation, and reflective practice; Leadership at the bedside and advocacy through everyday actions; Mary Adelaide Nutting's educational leadership and Luther Christman's advocacy for nursing roles and men in nursing.

Activity: Professional development plan: students identify strengths, growth areas, support resources, and habits that will help them succeed.

Assessment: Professional Development Plan

Week 12: Nursing Today and Your Future Impact

Focus: Students synthesize what they learned about nursing's past, present, and future while articulating the impact they hope to make as practical nurses.

Nursing leaders: Student-selected nursing leader review

Topics: Nursing today: technology, teamwork, evidence, patient rights, community needs, and lifelong learning; How practical nurses make an impact through small, consistent, safe actions; Review of ethical/legal foundations and professional identity; Student-selected leader presentations; Course synthesis and final exam preparation.

Activity: Final impact presentation: students connect one nursing leader, one ethical/legal responsibility, and one personal commitment to patient care.

Assessment: Final Exam and Final Impact Presentation
